lesson.learn:
Traits appear to skip generations when they are recessive and can hide inside carriers who do not show the trait themselves. A recessive allele can move quietly through a family until two copies meet in one child. Then the trait suddenly appears again, even though it looked absent for years.

This is not a trick of memory. It is the logic of inheritance. What seems to vanish is often still there, simply masked by a dominant allele or hidden in the family tree.
lesson.simpler: Some traits seem to skip generations because recessive versions can hide in carriers before showing up again later.
lesson.keyTakeaway: A trait can disappear from view without disappearing from the genes.
lesson.deeperDive:
Punnett squares and pedigree charts make the pattern easier to see. If both parents carry a recessive allele, their children can inherit the trait even when the parents do not show it. That is why families can be surprised by conditions that appear after several unaffected generations. The pattern is especially clear in single-gene traits, but real family history can get more complex when multiple genes or environment are involved.

This lesson is also a reminder that genetics is about probabilities, not certainty. Parents pass on alleles randomly, and the outcome for each child is a new draw from the same rules.
lesson.funFact: Carriers often look completely unaffected by recessive traits.

people: Genetic counselors - people who explain inheritance patterns
Children - where recessive traits can reappear
places: Family pedigrees - diagrams of inherited traits
Pea plant studies - the classic model system
Human families - where recessive conditions appear
Genetics clinics - where inheritance is explained
years: Present day - recessive inheritance is routinely tested
concepts: Recessive - needing two copies to show
Carrier - someone with one hidden copy
Allele - a version of a gene
